title: Rockliff spruiks 'exciting days ahead', but underneath, alarms wail
author: Josh  Duggan
fullText: There's always been something in Tasmanian politics for fans of the ABC's satire Utopia.The Tasmanian government has delivered again, with the name of its new infrastructure agency — 'Building Tasmania'.Look past its resemblance to Utopia's 'Nation Building Australia' — that's low-hanging fruit.Premier Jeremy Rockliff's agenda-setting State of the State speech, delivered yesterday, had enough diversion to make any writers' room swoon.The government responsible for buying two ferries without building a port for them put its infrastructure delivery credentials on full display, listing off the recent achievements.The now-secured Macquarie Point Stadium — and Tasmania Devils AFL and AFLW teams — was front and centre."We are a state entering a new era of opportunity and the foundations we have laid will build a prosperous future," Mr Rockliff said.The Macquarie Point stadium was pointed to as one sign of the state's "prosperous future". (Supplied: MPDC)He celebrated the decision to proceed with the Marinus Link undersea connector project.The agreement to refurbish the research ship Nuyina's home on the Hobart waterfront and keep the Australian Antarctic Division in Tasmania was lauded.The scandal-ridden Spirit of Tasmania project even got a mention, with the long-awaited ships set to sail up the Mersey this year."We all know the challenges we have faced with this massive undertaking. We said we would sort the Spirits, and that's exactly what we have done," Mr Rockliff said.All will be a boon for Tasmania, we're told."We have exciting days ahead of us … we have exciting years ahead of us."It all paints a rosy picture.You'd be forgiven for thinking there was no looming debt crisis, which is set to dominate discussion this year.The premier says the Spirits have been "sorted". (Rauma Marine Constructions)The state's ever-dizzying debt hole — and the tough calls the government needs to make to climb out of it — didn't get a mention until three-quarters of the way through.But that was quickly eschewed in favour of spruiking the proposed "total transformation" of the Department of State Growth.Replacing the Department of State Growth with "Building Tasmania" could save a quarter of a billion dollars a year, Mr Rockliff said.While the premier spoke of "right-sizing" the public service and a "reduction in headcount", the message sent to state servants was more direct about how many jobs were on the chopping block — 250 full-time positions.The first cut of many?Building Tasmania may well be exactly what is needed to "supercharge" delivery, as the premier says, but abolishing State Growth (and 250 jobs) is also a budget repair method.Make no mistake, 2026 will be the year of tough calls — take this announcement as the first of many.Treasury's Fiscal Sustainability Report, released last week, pulled no punches, warning without action, the net debt could blow out from $4 billion to $129.5 billion.It urged significant budget repair of about $3.3 billion over the next five years and $11.3 billion over 15 years.Treasury, along with economist Saul Eslake, has recommended a mix of expenditure cuts and new revenue-raising measures.The premier reiterated in his speech that May's budget will contain no new or increased taxes.There is some wriggle room there — this year's budget will contain a new "levy" on short-stay properties, which the Liberals committed to at the 2024 state election.But you can expect Treasurer Eric Abetz to do the heavy lifting of budget repair by cutting.The premier announced the abolition of the State Growth department, which will see public service jobs slashed. (Tasmanian government)The state government has made no secret of its ambition to cut more than 2,000 public servants, laying it out in successive budgets, though the size of the state service has actually continued to increase since then.The government has confirmed it's looking at slashing pension payments for high-earning public service retirees.There will be more tough calls on the way, and some might be harder to swallow.The Marinus Link undersea cable was also spruiked during the speech. (Prysmian Group)Affected workers 'shocked, scared'As for the announcement itself — abolishing State Growth — the battle lines are being drawn already.The state's peak business group, the Tasmanian Chamber of Commerce and Industry (TCCI), has long called for a slimmer public service.It says this department, ruled by seven ministers, was particularly difficult to work with.Labor and the Greens accused the premier of "running out of steam". (ABC News: Mackenzie Heard)The TCCI's Michael Bailey has backed this move, saying the "behemoth" department had been a source of more "confusion than clarity for a long time".But abolishing a department, and cutting loose a quarter of it's workforce, is no easy task.Community and Public Sector Union's Natalie Jones told ABC Hobart Drive her phone blew up with calls from members, who found out about the restructure shortly before parliament did."They're scared, they're shocked, they feel like they've already had years of reform," she told ABC Hobart Drive.If the dismantling of State Growth can be sold as slimming the bureaucracy, and driving efficiency, it may be easier to sell than cuts to nurses, police, teachers or child safety workersThough the Treasurer may want to tread carefully in this space, after his comments describing police as having a more "difficult" role than other public servants took on a life of their own.The opposition criticised the announcement yesterday — Labor Leader of Opposition Business Ella Haddad said it wasn't "a new initiative to help Tasmanians live their best lives".The Greens were dismayed at the lack of an agenda for the year ahead in the speech.Both accused the premier of "running out of steam", but there wasn't direct criticism from either of the move itself.The opposition and crossbench may be faced with the question of what budget repair they can support.Is there a moral imperative to put politics to the side and support some kind of budget repair, even if it's painful?There are more than enough tough calls to go around.
summary: For a debt-ridden state facing cuts to public service jobs, you'd hardly know it by listening to the rosy pictures being painted by Tasmania's premier.
